metadata.raw_response_text:
# The Keeper of the Halls

## How George Keeler became the most important person at Westfield High School without ever standing in front of a classroom

---

The first bell at Westfield High School rings at 7:25 a.m., but George Keeler has already been inside the building for three hours by then. He arrives in the dark, winter and summer alike, parking his truck in the same spot near the loading dock — the one with the small crack in the asphalt that he has reported to the district office annually since 2014. He pulls on his gray coveralls. He checks the boilers. He walks the halls with a flashlight before the fluorescents come on, listening to the building the way a doctor listens to a chest, alert to the subtle sounds that signal something is wrong. A hissing pipe. A stuck damper on the HVAC. The faint ticking of a ballast about to fail in a ceiling fixture above room 214.

"Buildings talk to you," Keeler says. "Most people just don't bother to listen."

He has been listening to this one for twenty-eight years.

---

George Robert Keeler, 61, is the head custodian of Westfield High School in Westfield, Ohio, a mid-sized public school with 1,100 students, 87 faculty and staff members, a three-story main building constructed in 1958, and a gymnasium annex added in 1991 that has never quite stopped leaking at the seam where the new roof meets the old. He is six feet tall, broad across the shoulders, with hands that look like they were designed for the specific purpose of turning wrenches and carrying buckets. His hair, once dark, has gone almost entirely silver. He wears wire-rimmed glasses that sit slightly crooked on his nose. When he smiles, which is often, deep lines fan out from the corners of his eyes like the pages of an open book.

He is, by almost every account, the most beloved person in the building.

Ask any student. They will tell you.

---

The thing people notice first about George Keeler — before his competence, before his kindness, before the fact that he can fix virtually anything with a short list of supplies and an almost supernatural patience — is that he knows their names.

All of them.

Not just the student body president or the starting quarterback or the girl who won the state science fair. All of them. The freshmen who arrived in August with bewildered expressions and schedules they couldn't read. The quiet kid in the back of third-period biology who hasn't spoken in class since September. The boy who eats lunch alone near the far stairwell, the one most teachers haven't even noticed yet.

George has noticed. George always notices.

"DeShawn," he'll say on a Tuesday morning, nodding at a sophomore walking past with his earbuds in. "How'd that math test go?"

DeShawn Hartley, 15, stops. Removes one earbud. Looks mildly amazed. "How'd you even know I had a math test?"

"You were studying in the cafeteria yesterday. Had your textbook out during lunch. Chapter nine. Quadratics."

DeShawn stares at him. "I got a B-plus."

"That's what I like to hear," George says, and keeps walking, pushing his wide dust mop down the corridor in long, practiced strokes.

---

The question everyone wants to ask — and many people do — is how. How does a man learn 1,100 names, every year, with roughly 275 new freshmen arriving each fall and a roughly equivalent number of seniors departing?

Keeler has a system, though he's reluctant to call it that. It sounds too formal for what is, at its core, an act of attention.

It starts in August, before school opens, when the freshman class roster is posted. Keeler gets a copy. He studies the names. When freshmen orientation happens, he stands near the front entrance, not behind a table, not wearing a name tag that says ASK ME — just standing there, in his coveralls, saying hello. He matches faces to the list he's been studying. He makes small associations in his mind, private mnemonics he won't fully share.

"I'll notice things," he says. "This one's got red sneakers. That one came in with her mom, and her mom looked more nervous than she did. This one's carrying a skateboard. You file it away."

Within three weeks, he has the freshmen down. Within six weeks, he rarely makes a mistake.

"The upperclassmen are easy," he says. "I've had three years with them already. It's the new ones that take work."

He downplays the effort. Those who know him say it is enormous.

---

Nancy Trujillo has taught English at Westfield for nineteen years, making her one of the longer-tenured teachers in the building — though still nine years short of Keeler's record. She remembers the moment she understood what he was doing.

"It was my second year," she says, sitting in her classroom after hours, papers fanned across her desk. "I had a student — I won't use her name — who was going through something terrible at home. She was withdrawing. Not turning in work. I was trying to figure out how to reach her, and I mentioned it in the teachers' lounge, not by name, just in general terms. George was fixing a cabinet hinge in the corner. He didn't say anything."

Two days later, Trujillo found a note in her mailbox. It was from Keeler, written on a torn piece of paper in his blocky handwriting. It said: *The girl you're worried about sits in the stairwell near the auditorium during her free period. She's been drawing in a sketchbook. She's good. Maybe that's a way in.*

"He didn't say her name," Trujillo says. "But he knew exactly who I was talking about. And he was right. I started the next conversation with her by asking about her drawings. It changed everything."

She pauses.

"I have a master's degree in education. I've been to dozens of professional development seminars on student engagement. And the most useful piece of pedagogical advice I've ever received was written on the back of a receipt by our custodian."

---

Keeler was not supposed to end up here. Or maybe he was. Depending on your philosophy about how lives unfold, his tenure at Westfield is either an accident or an inevitability.

He grew up in Westfield. Graduated from this same high school in 1982, walking across a stage in the gymnasium that, at the time, had a parquet floor. (It was replaced with hardwood in 1997. Keeler supervised the installation.) He was an average student by his own admission — C's and B's, decent in shop class, hopeless in chemistry. He played JV baseball for two years but wasn't good enough for varsity. He had no particular plans for after graduation.

"I wasn't one of those kids who knew what they wanted to be," he says. "I didn't have a calling. I had a diploma and a driver's license and not much else."

He worked construction for a few years. Did a stint at a warehouse. Married young — Linda, his high school girlfriend — and had two sons by the time he was twenty-five. When the school district posted an opening for a custodial position at Westfield High in 1996, he applied because the hours were steady, the benefits were decent, and Linda was pregnant with their third child, a daughter.

"I needed a paycheck," he says. "That's the honest truth. I wasn't looking for a purpose. I was looking for health insurance."

He got both.

---

His first year, he was assigned to the night shift, cleaning classrooms after the building emptied. He mopped floors, emptied trash cans, scrubbed desks, and buffed the hallway tile until it shone. He barely interacted with students. He barely interacted with anyone.

"I went home smelling like Pine-Sol," he says. "Linda made me shower before I could sit on the furniture."

After two years, he moved to the day shift when a position opened. And that, he says, is when everything changed.

"I was suddenly around kids all day. And I realized something pretty quick — a lot of these kids were invisible. Not to their friends, not to their teachers necessarily, but in the in-between spaces. The hallways. The cafeteria. Before school and after school. There were kids who moved through this building like ghosts."

He decided, without announcing it to anyone, that he would learn every student's name.

"It wasn't some grand plan," he says. "I just thought — if a kid walks past me every single day, the least I can do is know who they are."

---

Twenty-six years into his practice of learning names, Keeler has had roughly 7,700 students pass through his orbit. He doesn't keep count. He doesn't keep a spreadsheet. He does keep, in his small basement office next to the boiler room, a collection of items that students have given him over the years. A thank-you card from a girl who graduated in 2003 and is now a pediatrician in Columbus. A drawing from a boy with autism who spent forty-five minutes one afternoon watching Keeler repair a drinking fountain and then went home and drew the fountain, perfectly, from memory. A photograph of four members of the 2011 girls' basketball team, signed on the back: *To Mr. George, the real MVP.* A hand-painted coffee mug that says WORLD'S OKAYEST JANITOR, given to him by the class of 2018 as a joke. He uses it every day.

There is also a small framed letter, hanging on the wall near the light switch, that he does not readily discuss. It is from the parents of a boy named Caleb Marsh, a student who took his own life during his junior year in 2015. The letter thanks Keeler for being, in the parents' words, "one of the few adults in that school who saw our son, truly saw him, every single day." It thanks him for calling Caleb by name. For asking about his day. For noticing when he seemed down and telling him, simply, "I hope tomorrow's better, bud."

The letter does not blame the school. It does not blame anyone. It is a letter of gratitude, written in grief, and it is the only piece of paper in George Keeler's office that sits behind glass.

When asked about it, Keeler removes his glasses and rubs his eyes. His hands, for a moment, are not steady.

"You can do everything right," he says, "and still lose one. That's the part they don't tell you."

He puts his glasses back on. He clears his throat.

"But that's exactly why you keep trying. Because what if the next kid you say hello to — what if that's the moment that makes a difference? You don't know. You can never know. So you just keep showing up."

---

The building itself has become something like an extension of his body. He knows its rhythms the way a sailor knows a ship. He knows that the third-floor boys' bathroom has a toilet that runs unless you jiggle the handle to the left — not the right, the left. He knows that room 107 gets cold in January because the heating vent is partially blocked by ductwork that was improperly routed during a 2005 renovation. He has submitted a work order for this every year for nineteen years. He knows that the southeast stairwell door sticks in humid weather. He knows that if you stand in the main hallway at exactly 2:15 p.m. on a sunny afternoon, a beam of light comes through the transom window above the front entrance and falls directly on the trophy case, illuminating the 1974 state football championship trophy in a way that seems almost intentional. He discovered this by accident in 1999 and has never mentioned it to anyone.

He knows where the mice get in (a gap behind the kitchen's exterior exhaust fan) and where the roof leaks (three places, mapped with blue painter's tape in the attic crawl space). He knows the location of every circuit breaker, every shutoff valve, every fire extinguisher. He knows which brand of floor wax holds up under the traffic of 1,100 pairs of shoes per day and which brand is, in his words, "a waste of the taxpayers' money." He has opinions about floor wax. They are strongly held.

"People think custodial work is simple," he says. "And I guess on paper it is. You clean things. You fix things. But a school isn't a warehouse. It's not an office building. It's a living thing. It's got a thousand people in it with a thousand problems, and the building is either working for them or it's working against them. My job is to make sure it's working for them."

---

Principal Diane Okafor-Williams, who has led Westfield for the past six years, calls Keeler "the institutional memory of this school."

"Administrators come and go. Teachers retire and new ones take their place. The student body turns over completely every four years. George is the constant. He is the thread that connects 1996 to today."

She recounts an incident from her first week as principal, when she found Keeler in the hallway at 6:30 a.m., running his hand along a wall.

"I asked him what he was doing. He said, 'Checking for moisture.' I said, 'Is there a leak?' He said, 'Not yet.' He could feel something in the wall — some change in temperature or texture — that told him a pipe fitting was starting to fail behind the drywall. He was right. We caught it before it flooded the teachers' lounge. That would have been a $15,000 repair. Instead, it was a $200 fix."

She shakes her head.

"That man has saved this district more money than anyone will ever calculate. And that's the least important thing about him."

---

It is a Wednesday in late October, and George Keeler is standing at the intersection of the main hallway and the science wing, the spot he calls "the crossroads," where virtually every student passes at least once during the day. The bell has just rung for the transition between second and third period, and the hallway has erupted into noise and motion.

Keeler stands with his mop handle resting against his shoulder like a staff. He nods. He waves. He speaks.

"Morning, Ayana."

"Hey, Marcus. Tuck that shirt in before Okafor sees you."

"Jade! How's the ankle? You back at practice yet?"

"What's up, Trevor. Tell your mom I said thanks for the banana bread."

"Mia. Mia. Hey — slow down. The room's not going anywhere."

A small freshman boy with an oversized backpack rounds the corner too fast and nearly collides with him. Keeler doesn't flinch. He puts out one hand to steady the boy and says, calmly, "Easy, Rafael. You got time."

Rafael grins, adjusts his backpack straps, and continues down the hall at a marginally reduced speed.

In the span of four minutes, Keeler has greeted roughly thirty students by name. Not one of them looked surprised. This is simply what happens at the crossroads between second and third period. It has been happening, in some form, for more than a quarter of a century.

---

There is an argument to be made — and some have made it — that what Keeler does should be studied, replicated, formalized into some kind of program. At a 2019 school board meeting, a member proposed a "know your students" initiative modeled on Keeler's approach. Keeler was invited to attend. He sat in the back row in a clean pair of coveralls, visibly uncomfortable.

When asked to speak, he stood and said: "I appreciate the thought, but you can't make a program out of giving a damn. Either you care enough to learn a kid's name or you don't."

He sat back down. The initiative was approved in a diluted form — laminated name tags for freshmen during the first week of school — and was quietly abandoned by October.

Keeler does not bring this up. He does not gloat. He does not seem interested in being right. He is only interested in being present, which is a different and rarer thing.

---

His own children — George Jr., 35; Michael, 33; and Sarah, 28 — all attended Westfield while he worked there. They have complicated feelings about this.

"It was weird," says Michael Keeler, who now teaches fourth grade in a neighboring district. "On one hand, my dad was this beloved figure who everyone knew. Random kids would come up to me and say, 'Your dad's awesome.' On the other hand, you can't get away with anything when your father is literally in the building all day. I couldn't skip class. I couldn't slack off. He'd know before I got home."

Sarah Keeler, who works as a social worker in Cleveland, offers a different perspective.

"I think what I understand now, as an adult, is that my dad gave a version of himself to every kid in that school that most people only give to their own children. He paid attention to them. He remembered things about their lives. He worried about them. And I used to be jealous of that, honestly, when I was a teenager. I thought — why do these strangers get my dad's attention? But now I understand. It wasn't that he had less attention for us. It was that he had more attention than most people. Enough to go around."

She pauses.

"He taught me what I do now. I just didn't realize it until I was in graduate school."

---

There are things that George Keeler does not discuss on the record. His salary, for one, though public records show that the average custodian in the district earns between $32,000 and $41,000 per year, a figure that has not kept pace with inflation and that Keeler finds "disappointing but not surprising." His health, for another — he had a knee replacement in 2020 and was back at work in five weeks, three weeks ahead of his doctor's recommendation and two weeks behind his own. The future, for a third. He is sixty-one years old. Retirement is a topic that his wife raises periodically and that he deflects with the practiced ease of a man who has spent nearly three decades navigating hallways full of teenagers.

"I'll know when it's time," he says.

Linda Keeler, reached by phone, laughs at this.

"He will not know when it's time," she says. "That building will have to fall down around him. And even then he'd probably stay to sweep up."

---

On a Friday afternoon in November, after the final bell, the hallways empty with their usual speed. Within fifteen minutes, the building that held 1,100 young lives is quiet. Keeler begins his afternoon rounds, checking rooms, picking up stray papers, straightening desks that have migrated out of formation during the day's lessons.

In a second-floor classroom, he finds a notebook left behind on a desk. He picks it up, flips to the inside cover, and reads the name written there in blue ink.

"Olivia Chen," he says aloud. "Room 206. She's got Mr. Patterson for history, fourth period. She'll be looking for this on Monday."

He tucks the notebook under his arm and continues down the hall. He will place it in the lost-and-found bin near the main office, but he will also, on Monday morning, find Olivia Chen and tell her where it is. Because that is what he does. He notices. He remembers. He pays attention to the small things that, for a teenager navigating the chaos of high school, are not small at all.

The hallway is silent. His footsteps echo on the tile — tile he has mopped thousands of times, tile that bears the ghostly scuff marks of twenty-eight years' worth of students who came through this building and were known, by at least one person, by name.

He turns off the lights behind him as he goes.

---

*George Keeler will report to work on Monday at 4:30 a.m. He will check the boilers. He will walk the halls. And when the first students arrive, blinking and half-awake, clutching coffee they're technically not supposed to have, he will be standing at the crossroads, ready.*

*He will know their names.*

A small freshman boy with an oversized backpack rounds the corner too fast and nearly collides with him. Keeler doesn't flinch. He puts out one hand to steady the boy and says, calmly, "Easy, Rafael. You got time."

Rafael grins, adjusts his backpack straps, and continues down the hall at a marginally reduced speed.

In the span of four minutes, Keeler has greeted roughly thirty students by name. Not one of them looked surprised. This is simply what happens at the crossroads between second and third period. It has been happening, in some form, for more than a quarter of a century.

---

There is an argument to be made — and some have made it — that what Keeler does should be studied, replicated, formalized into some kind of program. At a 2019 school board meeting, a member proposed a "know your students" initiative modeled on Keeler's approach. Keeler was invited to attend. He sat in the back row in a clean pair of coveralls, visibly uncomfortable.

When asked to speak, he stood and said: "I appreciate the thought, but you can't make a program out of giving a damn. Either you care enough to learn a kid's name or you don't."

He sat back down. The initiative was approved in a diluted form — laminated name tags for freshmen during the first week of school — and was quietly abandoned by October.

Keeler does not bring this up. He does not gloat. He does not seem interested in being right. He is only interested in being present, which is a different and rarer thing.

---

His own children — George Jr., 35; Michael, 33; and Sarah, 28 — all attended Westfield while he worked there. They have complicated feelings about this.

"It was weird," says Michael Keeler, who now teaches fourth grade in a neighboring district. "On one hand, my dad was this beloved figure who everyone knew. Random kids would come up to me and say, 'Your dad's awesome.' On the other hand, you can't get away with anything when your father is literally in the building all day. I couldn't skip class. I couldn't slack off. He'd know before I got home."

Sarah Keeler, who works as a social worker in Cleveland, offers a different perspective.

"I think what I understand now, as an adult, is that my dad gave a version of himself to every kid in that school that most people only give to their own children. He paid attention to them. He remembered things about their lives. He worried about them. And I used to be jealous of that, honestly, when I was a teenager. I thought — why do these strangers get my dad's attention? But now I understand. It wasn't that he had less attention for us. It was that he had more attention than most people. Enough to go around."

She pauses.

"He taught me what I do now. I just didn't realize it until I was in graduate school."

---

There are things that George Keeler does not discuss on the record. His salary, for one, though public records show that the average custodian in the district earns between $32,000 and $41,000 per year, a figure that has not kept pace with inflation and that Keeler finds "disappointing but not surprising." His health, for another — he had a knee replacement in 2020 and was back at work in five weeks, three weeks ahead of his doctor's recommendation and two weeks behind his own. The future, for a third. He is sixty-one years old. Retirement is a topic that his wife raises periodically and that he deflects with the practiced ease of a man who has spent nearly three decades navigating hallways full of teenagers.

"I'll know when it's time," he says.

Linda Keeler, reached by phone, laughs at this.

"He will not know when it's time," she says. "That building will have to fall down around him. And even then he'd probably stay to sweep up."

---

On a Friday afternoon in November, after the final bell, the hallways empty with their usual speed. Within fifteen minutes, the building that held 1,100 young lives is quiet. Keeler begins his afternoon rounds, checking rooms, picking up stray papers, straightening desks that have migrated out of formation during the day's lessons.

In a second-floor classroom, he finds a notebook left behind on a desk. He picks it up, flips to the inside cover, and reads the name written there in blue ink.

"Olivia Chen," he says aloud. "Room 206. She's got Mr. Patterson for history, fourth period. She'll be looking for this on Monday."

He tucks the notebook under his arm and continues down the hall. He will place it in the lost-and-found bin near the main office, but he will also, on Monday morning, find Olivia Chen and tell her where it is. Because that is what he does. He notices. He remembers. He pays attention to the small things that, for a teenager navigating the chaos of high school, are not small at all.

The hallway is silent. His footsteps echo on the tile — tile he has mopped thousands of times, tile that bears the ghostly scuff marks of twenty-eight years' worth of students who came through this building and were known, by at least one person, by name.

He turns off the lights behind him as he goes.

---

*George Keeler will report to work on Monday at 4:30 a.m. He will check the boilers. He will walk the halls. And when the first students arrive, blinking and half-awake, clutching coffee they're technically not supposed to have, he will be standing at the crossroads, ready.*

*He will know their names.*
